title: Grass on White Cotton — Three Components, Three Chemistry Steps
stainChemistry: Grass is a three-component combination stain: (1) chlorophyll — the green pigment, a fat-soluble porphyrin ring compound that dissolves in alcohol or organic solvents but NOT in water; (2) tannin polyphenols — water-soluble plant compounds that bond to cellulose through hydrogen bonds; (3) plant protein — amino acids and peptides from crushed plant cells that bond to cotton. The green color comes from chlorophyll, but the persistent stain after the green fades is from tannin and protein. This is why grass doesn't wash out easily — you're fighting three different chemistries simultaneously.
whyThisWorks: The sequence follows combination stain protocol: treat protein first (cold, before heat), then tannin, then the chlorophyll color. On white cotton, you have the full arsenal available. Protease enzyme handles the plant protein. Isopropyl alcohol or amyl acetate dissolves the chlorophyll (which is oil-soluble). Tannin formula or acid addresses the polyphenol component. Peroxide or oxygen bleach cleans up any residual green shadow. Dan Eisen classifies grass as a combination tannin+protein stain, and the GONR sequence rules specify: protein first (cold) → tannin → chlorophyll → bleach.

spottingProtocol.technique: Flush from back with water gun
spottingProtocol.temperature: Cold — protein component must be addressed before heat
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush with cold water from the back. This removes the water-soluble components (plant juices, some tannin) and begins loosening the protein. Cold water is mandatory because grass contains protein — heat sets it. On cotton, normal pressure is fine.

spottingProtocol.agent: Protease enzyme digester OR enzyme detergent (warm, 100–110°F)
spottingProtocol.temperature: Warm — enzyme activation temperature. Safe after cold pre-flush removed surface protein.
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply enzyme solution to the grass stain. Protease digests the plant protein component — this is safe on cotton (cellulose fiber). On the spotting board, use Prochem Power Burst diluted per label in warm water. Tamp with brush to drive enzyme into the fabric. Let it work 15–20 minutes. At home, soak the stained area in Tide + warm water for 30 minutes.

spottingProtocol.agent: Isopropyl alcohol 90% OR A.L. Wilson Formula 83 VDS
spottingProtocol.technique: Apply to green chlorophyll residue, blot toward towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: After enzyme treatment, flush and inspect. If green color remains, it's chlorophyll — fat-soluble, water-insoluble. Apply isopropyl alcohol (professional: use Formula 83 VDS) to the green area. Work from outside in. The alcohol dissolves chlorophyll's porphyrin ring structure. Blot toward the towel beneath — you should see green transfer. Repeat until green transfer stops. On the spotting board, flush with VDS after treatment.

spottingProtocol.agent: Tannin formula (acetic acid-based) OR white vinegar
spottingProtocol.temperature: Ambient — can warm slightly on cotton
spottingProtocol.instruction: After chlorophyll and protein are removed, any remaining tan/brown discoloration is from tannin polyphenols. Apply tannin formula (StreeTAN or equivalent) or dilute acetic acid. The acid breaks hydrogen bonds between tannin compounds and cellulose. On cotton, this is straightforward. Tamp with brush, dwell 3–5 minutes, flush.

spottingProtocol.technique: Complete flush between tannin formula and oxidizer
spottingProtocol.instruction: CRITICAL: Flush the tannin formula completely before applying peroxide in the next step. If tannin formula is still in the fiber when peroxide hits, it oxidizes the tannin IN PLACE — creating a darker, permanent compound. This is the most common professional mistake with tannin stains.

spottingProtocol.temperature: Warm — steam safe on white cotton after protein and tannin treatment
spottingProtocol.instruction: For any remaining color shadow — green from chlorophyll residue or brown from tannin. Apply 6% peroxide with ammonia catalyst. Steam-chase to activate. On white cotton, you can be aggressive here — no dye to worry about. The peroxide oxidizes remaining chromophores. Watch the color fade.
Neutralize alkaline residue with dilute acetic acid. Flush thoroughly. Machine dry safe only after confirming stain is fully removed. Air dry if any doubt — heat sets residual tannin and protein.

homeSolutions: FRESH: Soak in cold water + enzyme detergent (Tide Original) for 30 minutes. The enzyme handles the protein, the surfactant handles some of the chlorophyll. Wash in warm water. Inspect before drying. This handles most fresh grass stains.

MODERATE/DRIED: Pre-treat with rubbing alcohol (90% isopropyl) — apply directly to the green chlorophyll stain, blot with white cloth. You'll see green transfer to the cloth. Repeat until transfer stops. Then soak in cold water + Tide for 30 minutes. Wash warm.

STUBBORN GREEN ON WHITE: After enzyme soak + alcohol treatment, apply 3% hydrogen peroxide to any remaining green shadow. Let sit 10 minutes on white cotton. Rinse. Then OxiClean soak (per label in warm water) for 2–4 hours. Wash warm. This combination addresses all three components.

BASEBALL PANTS SPECIAL: The nuclear option for grass-stained white baseball pants — soak in cold water + Tide for 1 hour, then scrub the green areas with rubbing alcohol + old toothbrush. Rinse. Make an OxiClean paste (powder + just enough water), apply to remaining green, let sit 2 hours. Wash warm with Tide + 1/2 cup OxiClean powder. Inspect before drying.
materialWarnings: NEVER hot water first — grass contains protein that heat sets permanently.
NEVER apply peroxide while tannin formula is still wet in the fiber — creates a permanent dark compound.
NEVER use the dryer until stain is confirmed gone — heat sets both protein and tannin components.
NEVER skip the alcohol step for green color — grass chlorophyll is oil-soluble and does NOT dissolve in water or detergent alone. You need a solvent.

professional.name: Prochem Power Burst
professional.use: Enzyme prespray for the protein component — first chemical step
professional.note: Protease + amylase. Warm water, dwell 15–20 minutes. Cotton-safe — enzyme only attacks protein, not cellulose.

professional.name: A.L. Wilson Formula 83 VDS
professional.use: Volatile dry solvent for chlorophyll dissolution
professional.note: Apply to green residue after enzyme treatment. VDS dissolves chlorophyll and evaporates clean — no ring marks. Alternative: amyl acetate for stubborn chlorophyll.

professional.name: Street's Tannin Remover (StreeTAN)
professional.use: Tannin polyphenol component — third chemistry step
professional.note: Acidic tannin formula. Apply after chlorophyll is dissolved. Dwell 3–5 minutes on cotton, flush thoroughly before oxidizer step.

professional.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 6%
professional.use: Final chromophore bleaching for residual green or brown on white cotton
professional.note: With ammonia catalyst. Steam-chase on white cotton. Safe and effective for final cleanup.

consumer.name: Tide Original Liquid
consumer.use: Enzyme detergent soak for protein component
consumer.note: Cold to warm water, soak 30 minutes minimum. Handles the plant protein that makes grass stains stick.

consumer.name: Isopropyl Alcohol 90%
consumer.use: Chlorophyll dissolver — the key ingredient most people don't use
consumer.note: Apply directly to green stain, blot with white cloth. The reason grass 'won't come out in the wash' is that chlorophyll is oil-soluble — water and detergent can't dissolve it. Alcohol can.

consumer.name: OxiClean Versatile Stain Remover
consumer.use: Oxygen bleach for final green shadow + tannin residue
consumer.note: Make a paste for direct stain treatment, or dissolve per label for soaking. Effective on all three grass components with extended dwell time.

consumer.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3%
consumer.use: Spot bleaching for green residue on white cotton
consumer.note: Apply directly to remaining green after alcohol and enzyme treatment. Safe on white cotton. Test on colors.

escalation.when: Grass stain persists after enzyme + alcohol + tannin + peroxide sequence, indicating the chlorophyll has polymerized from heat exposure or the tannin has oxidized from age. Also escalate for grass stains on colored cotton where bleaching options are limited.
escalation.whatToTell: Tell the cleaner: 'This is grass on white cotton. I treated with enzyme detergent and rubbing alcohol for the chlorophyll. A [green/brown] shadow remains. The stain is approximately [X] days old. I did not use heat before treatment.'
escalation.specialistType: Any competent dry cleaner should handle grass on cotton. Wet cleaning is the ideal method. For grass on delicate fabrics or colored cotton, a cleaner with wet cleaning capability and tannin removal experience is preferred.

solventNote: For dry cleaners: Grass is a combination stain requiring both dry-side and wet-side treatment. Start dry-side: VDS or POG for chlorophyll (dissolves in organic solvent). Then wet-side: protein formula for plant protein, tannin formula for polyphenols, peroxide for residual color. In the machine, PERC and HC will dissolve chlorophyll but won't address the protein and tannin — those must be pre-spotted wet-side. On white cotton, post-spot with peroxide+ammonia+steam for any remaining shadow.
